name: Midir
description: Midir is a prominent figure in Irish mythology, often depicted as a king of the Tuatha Dé Danann, a supernatural race inhabiting Ireland before the arrival of the Gaels. He is associated with the Otherworld, specifically with the síd (fairy mound) of Brí Léith, which he rules. Midir is known for his beauty, wisdom, and magical abilities. He is a powerful warrior and a skilled shapeshifter, capable of transforming himself and others. One of the most famous stories involving Midir is his abduction of Étaín, the wife of Eochaid Airem, the High King of Ireland. Through cunning and magic, Midir regains Étaín, who had been reincarnated and married to Eochaid. This tale highlights Midir's persistence, his connection to the Otherworld, and the complexities of love and fate in Irish mythology. He is often seen as a figure of both power and intrigue, representing the allure and dangers of the Otherworld. Midir's interactions with mortals and other deities demonstrate the constant interplay between the human and supernatural realms in Irish folklore. His legacy continues to influence modern interpretations of Irish mythology, serving as a reminder of the enduring power of the ancient stories.
subtitle: King of the Sí, Shapeshifter

keyMyths.title: The Wooing of Étaín
keyMyths.description: Midir wins Étaín in a chess match but must wait a year to claim her. He eventually abducts her during a feast, flying away as swans.

keyMyths.title: Midir's Challenge to Eochaid
keyMyths.description: Midir appears at Eochaid's court disguised as a beggar and challenges him to a series of chess games, winning each time and demanding increasing favors.

keyMyths.title: The Shape-Shifting Contest
keyMyths.description: Midir engages in a shape-shifting contest with Eochaid, transforming into various animals and ultimately into swans to escape with Étaín.
